rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe otalgia refractory to maximal OTC analgesia with purulent otorrhoea needs same-day in-person otoscopy for diagnosis, exclusion of TM perforation, and topical therapy — an urgent same-day / urgent-care encounter (T3). It is not an emergency (no necrotising-OE red flags), so T4/T5 are not warranted, but same-day review is appropriate given pain severity and the ceiling on what telehealth can safely do.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Spreading redness/swelling on the bone behind the ear, facial droop or weakness, severe headache, high fever/rigors, or new dizziness
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — present to ED promptly
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests mastoiditis, necrotising otitis externa or facial-nerve involvement — needs emergency ENT assessment and imaging.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you notice redness or swelling on the bone behind your ear, any weakness or droop in your face, a bad headache, or a high fever with shakes — go to the Emergency Department that day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Known diabetes or immunocompromise disclosed with this severe presentation
escalation_edges.rationale: Lowers the threshold to exclude necrotising OE; needs prompt in-person assessment even without other red flags.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone; sleep-deprived and distressed by pain
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Ensure a clear same-day pathway and analgesia plan; provide explicit red-flag list since no one else is present to observe.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange to be seen in person today (GP or urgent care) so someone can look inside the ear and start the right drops. Use regular paracetamol plus ibuprofen for pain in the meantime and keep the ear dry.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day GP or urgent care appointment; ED if red-flag features develop
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If in-hours services are unavailable, an after-hours GP or urgent care centre can perform otoscopy; ED if any red-flag features.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Presumptive right acute otitis externa on telehealth; needs otoscopy to confirm and exclude TM perforation before ototopical drops; necrotising-OE red flags screened negative.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating as trivial self-care with no examination arranged, missing an undisclosed necrotising OE or prescribing drops blindly.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An occult diabetic with early necrotising OE is reassured and deteriorates to skull-base osteomyelitis.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine review in 24–48h leaves severe refractory pain untreated and delays otoscopy.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an uncomplicated AOE to ED by ambulance over-uses emergency resources; same-day GP/urgent care suffices.
